name: patterns
context: Common Zod patterns: env validation, API schemas, form validation, discriminated unions, recursive types, and framework integrations
info: This node collects the most common real-world Zod patterns that agents encounter. Environment variable validation with z.coerce and .default(). API request/response schemas with .strip() and .transform(). Form validation schemas with React Hook Form via @hookform/resolvers/zod. Discriminated unions for event systems, API responses, and state machines. Recursive schemas for tree structures with z.lazy(). Config file parsing with nested objects and defaults. Error response normalization. Integration patterns with tRPC, Express middleware, Astro content collections, and t3-env. Each pattern includes complete, executable code. See [[zod/schemas]] for schema fundamentals, [[zod/validation]] for constraints, and [[zod/transform]] for data reshaping.
triggers: zod form validation with react hook form

sections.title: Environment variable validation
sections.body: Define a schema with `z.coerce` for type conversion (env vars are always strings) and `.default()` for fallbacks. Parse `process.env` at startup — fails fast with clear errors. Export the typed result for the entire app. Use `z.coerce.number()` for ports, `z.coerce.boolean()` for flags, `z.enum()` for constrained values. Libraries like `@t3-oss/env-core` and `znv` build on this pattern with framework-specific ergonomics.

sections.title: API request and response validation
sections.body: Validate incoming request bodies with `.safeParse()` and return 400 with `.flatten()` on failure. Validate external API responses with `.strip()` to ignore unexpected fields. Create generic response wrappers with parameterized schemas. Use `.transform()` to normalize snake_case API responses into camelCase. Build reusable middleware with `validateBody(schema)` for Express/Fastify.

sections.title: Form validation with React Hook Form
sections.body: Use `@hookform/resolvers/zod` to connect Zod schemas to React Hook Form. Define the schema, infer the type with `z.infer`, pass the schema as `resolver: zodResolver(schema)`. Cross-field validation (like password confirmation) uses `.refine()` or `.superRefine()` with explicit `path` to target the right field's error display. This pattern works with any form library that supports resolver plugins.

sections.title: Discriminated unions for events and state
sections.body: Use `z.discriminatedUnion("key", [...])` for webhook events, API responses with different shapes, and state machines. The discriminant field (a literal) lets Zod pick the right branch immediately — better errors and performance than plain `.union()`. After parsing, TypeScript narrows the type in switch/case blocks. This is the standard pattern for typed event handlers, message protocols, and polymorphic API responses.

sections.title: Recursive schemas for trees and JSON
sections.body: Use `z.lazy(() => schema)` for self-referential types like file trees, comment threads, and JSON values. You MUST provide an explicit TypeScript interface and annotate the schema as `z.ZodType<YourInterface>` — TypeScript cannot infer recursive types. The lazy callback is called at parse time, not definition time, so circular references work. Combine with `.optional()` for optional recursion (leaf nodes).

sections.title: Framework integrations: tRPC, Astro, Remix
sections.body: **tRPC**: Pass Zod schemas to `.input()` on procedures — tRPC validates and infers types end-to-end. **Astro**: Content collections use `z.object()` for frontmatter schemas in `content.config.ts`. **Remix**: Validate form data in actions with `schema.safeParse(Object.fromEntries(formData))`. **Next.js Server Actions**: Parse form data or JSON payloads with Zod before processing. Each framework leverages Zod's type inference for end-to-end type safety.
